Item 1. Business.
The Procter & Gamble Company (the Company) is a world-leading multinational consumer goods company focused on providing trusted, branded products of superior quality, performance and value to improve the lives of consumers around the world - now and for generations to come. Our products are sold in about 180 countries and territories throughout the world. The Company was incorporated in Ohio in 1905, having first been established as a New Jersey corporation in 1890, and was built from a business founded in Cincinnati in 1837 by William Procter and James Gamble.
Additional information required by this item is incorporated herein by reference to Management's Discussion and Analysis (MD&A); and Notes 1 and 2 to our Consolidated Fina
…[truncated 5392 chars]…
 do business. In the United States, many of our products and manufacturing operations are subject to one or more federal or state regulatory agencies, including the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA), the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA), the Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA), the Federal Trade Commission (FTC) and the Consumer Product Safety Commission (CPSC). We are also subject to anti-corruption laws and regulations, such as the U.S. Foreign Corrupt Practices Act, and antitrust and competition laws and regulations that govern our dealings with suppliers, customers, competitors and government officials.
In addition, many foreign jurisdictions in which we do business have regulations and regulatory bodies that govern similar aspects of our operations and products, in some cases to an even more significant degree. We are also subject to expanding laws and regulations related to environmental protection and other sustainability-related matters, non-financial reporting and diligence, labor and employment, trade, taxation and privacy and data protection, including the European Union's General Data Protection Regulation and similar regulations in states within the United States and in countries around the world.
The Company has in place compliance programs and internal and external experts to help guide our business in complying with these and other existing laws and regulations that apply to us around the globe; and we have made, and plan to continue making, necessary expenditures for compliance with these laws and regulations. We also expect that our many suppliers, consultants and other third parties working on our behalf share our commitment to compliance, and we have policies and procedures in place to manage these relationships, though they inherently involve a lesser degree of control over operations and governance. We do not expect that the Company's expenditures for compliance with current government regulations, including current environmental regulations, will have a material effect on our total capital expenditures, earnings or competitive position in fiscal year 2026 as compared to prior periods.
Human Capital
. Our employees are a key source of competitive advantage. Their actions, guided by our Purpose, Values and Principles (PVPs), are critical to the long-term success of our business. We aim to retain our talented employees by offering competitive compensation and benefits, strong career development and a respectful and inclusive culture that provides equal opportunity for all.
Our Board of Directors, through the Compensation and Leadership Development Committee (C&LD Committee), provides oversight of the Company's policies and strategy relating to talent, including equality and inclusion, as well as the Company's compensation principles and practices. The C&LD Committee also evaluates and approves the Company's compensation plans, policies and programs applicable to our senior executives.
Employees
As of June 30, 2025, the Company had approximately 109,000 employees, an increase of 2% versus the prior year due to business growth. The total number of employees is an estimate of total Company employees excluding interns, co-ops, contractors and employees of joint ventures. 49% of our employees are in manufacturing roles and 28% of our employees are located in the United States. 42% of our global employees are women and 32% of our U.S. employees identify as multicultural.
Training and Development
We focus on attracting, developing and retaining the broadest pool of talent available, both from universities and the broader market. We recruit from universities across markets in which we compete and are generally able to select from the top talent. We focus on developing our employees by providing a variety of job experiences, training programs and skill development opportunities. Given our develop-from-within model for staffing most of our senior leadership positions, it is particularly important for us to ensure holistic growth and full engagement of our employees.
Equality and Inclusion
As a global consumer products company, P&G serves consumers around the world with operations in approximately 70 countries. We believe that it is good for business that our workforce is drawn from the best available talent from communities worldwide, with insights about, connectivity to and understanding of all our consumers. Further, a foundational component of P&G's strategy and success has been to foster an inclusive work environment, in which we develop and advance the very best capabilities that all our people have to offer. Globally, we aspire to reflect the diversity of the consumers we serve in the communities where we operate and are committed to creating a superior employee experience for all. Our aspiration is founded on our longstanding values and principles for equal opportunity and compliance with the law.
Compensation and Benefits
Market-competitive compensation and reward programs are critical elements of our employee value equation to attract and retain the best talent. Our total rewards programs are based on the principles of paying for performance, paying competitively versus peer companies that we compete with for talent in the marketplace and focusing on long-term success through a combination of short-term and long-term incentive programs. We also offer competitive benefit programs, including retirement plans and health insurance, in line with local country practices, with flexibility to accommodate the needs of a diverse workforce.
Sustainability
. Environmental sustainability is integrated into our business strategy. We are focused on designing and manufacturing irresistibly superior products that are more sustainable. We aim to reduce our own environmental footprint and
The Procter & Gamble Company        3
enable our consumers to reduce their footprint without compromising on the performance of the products they use. We develop and license technologies that can be used across industries to improve environmental sustainability at a broader scale.
Combined, this approach intends to positively impact the total environmental impact of the Company while driving market growth and value creation.
In 2021, the Company announced a 2040 net zero ambition. Our Climate Transition Action Plan outlines the Company's ongoing efforts toward reducing greenhouse gas emissions across scopes 1 and 2 and elements of scope 3. The Company has also declared ambitions towards purchasing renewable electricity for our operations, reducing intensity of virgin petroleum-based plastic in packaging, designing more consumer packaging to be recyclable or reusable, responsible sourcing of key forest-based commodities, improving efficiency of water usage in our operations and driving a global portfolio of water restoration projects in key water basins. While we have met or exceeded some of the goals and are making significant progress against others, scalable infrastructure and cost-effective solutions are not yet available to us to fully deliver against some of our stated ambitions.
Sustainability related disclosures included in this Annual Report, our Proxy Statement and our sustainability reports are informed by standards and guidelines such as the Global Reporting Initiative (GRI) and the Task Force on Climate-related Financial Disclosures (TCFD). The "materiality" thresholds in those standards and guidelines may differ from the concept of "materiality" for purposes of the federal securities laws and disclosures required by the Commission's rules in this Annual Report. References to our sustainability reports and website are for informational purposes only and neither the sustainability reports nor the other information on our website is incorporated by reference into this Annual Report on Form 10-K. Additional detailed information on our sustainability efforts can be found on our website at https://pginvestor.com/esg.

**Quantitative Report: Procter & Gamble (PG)**
**Decision Date: 2025-10-01 | All evidence point-in-time before this date.**

**1. Revenue, Earnings, Margin, Cash-Flow, and Balance-Sheet Trajectory**
*   **Revenue:** Latest quarter (Q3 2025-03-31) revenue was $19.78B, a year-over-year (YoY) decline of 2.1% (from $20.20B) and a sequential (QoQ) decline of 9.6% (from $21.88B). Trailing Twelve Months (TTM) revenue was $83.93B.
*   **Earnings:** Latest quarter diluted EPS was $1.54, a YoY increase of 1.3% (from $1.52) but a QoQ decline of 18.1% (from $1.88). TTM net income was $15.58B.
*   **Margins:** Latest quarter gross margin was 51.0% ($10.08B/$19.78B), operating margin was 23.1% ($4.56B/$19.78B), and net margin was 19.2% ($3.79B/$19.78B).
*   **Cash-Flow:** No cash flow statement data is provided.
*   **Balance Sheet:** Total equity increased to $52.55B at Q3 2025 from $48.01B at Q3 2023. The debt-to-equity ratio (Total Liabilities/Total Equity) was 1.34 ($70.44B / $52.55B) as of Q3 2025.

**2. Earnings Quality and Balance-Sheet Resilience**
*   **Earnings Quality:** Cannot be fully assessed due to the absence of cash flow data. Net income margins have been relatively stable between 0.172 and 0.186 over the lookback period. TTM net income grew 0.3% YoY.
*   **Balance-Sheet Resilience:** Total assets remained stable between $119.60B and $126.48B over the seven-quarter period. Total equity has shown a gradual upward trend, indicating retained earnings accumulation.

**3. Valuation versus Company History**
*   The stock's latest Price-to-Earnings (Diluted) ratio was 24.155 (as of 2025-09-30). This sits at the lower end of its one-year range of 23.7 to 30.8.
*   The Price-to-Book ratio was 7.164, also near the lower end of its one-year range of 7.0 to 8.5.
*   The earnings yield (E/P) was 0.041 (4.1%). Valuation multiples have compressed over the past year, with the PE ratio declining from 28.744 in October 2024 to the current 24.155.

**4. Price Level and Returns Context**
*   The closing price on 2025-09-30 was $153.65. The 52-week high was $180.43 and the low was $149.91.
*   The stock has generated negative returns: a quarterly return (approx. 63 days) of -4.7% and an annual return (approx. 252 days) of -11.3%.
*   The quarterly path shows a persistent negative trend over the last four periods. The annual price decline of -11.3% has occurred alongside relatively stable net margins and a slight YoY increase in EPS.

**5. Strongest Quantitative Upside and Downside**
*   **Upside:** The primary quantitative upside is valuation reversion. The PE multiple of 24.155 is at the bottom of its one-year range. A return to the middle of the range (~27.3) would imply a ~13% price increase, assuming constant earnings.
*   **Downside:** The primary quantitative downside is fundamental deterioration. The latest quarter showed significant sequential declines in revenue (-9.6%) and earnings (-18.1%). A continuation of this trend could further pressure the stock price, independent of valuation.

**6. Provisional Quantitative Risk/Reward Conclusion**
*   The quantitative profile presents a mixed risk/reward. The stock is trading at a historically low valuation (PE 24.155) which provides some downside cushion and potential upside if multiples revert. However, the recent fundamental trajectory is weak, with clear sequential declines in key income statement metrics. The absence of cash flow data is a significant gap in assessing quality. The price decline has outpaced the modest YoY earnings growth, indicating investor concern about the recent trajectory. A provisional conclusion based solely on supplied numbers suggests a balanced risk/reward with a slight negative bias due to the unfavorable recent fundamental momentum.
PG.qualitative_report:
### Qualitative Equity Report: Procter & Gamble (PG)
**Decision Date:** 2025-10-01

**1. Business/Franchise Read**
PG is a world-leading multinational consumer goods company operating in approximately 180 countries. Its business model is centered on creating, manufacturing, marketing, and distributing a diversified portfolio of daily-use branded products across key categories (specifics referenced in financial statements). The company emphasizes "irresistible superiority" in product performance, packaging, communication, retail execution, and value. It relies on continuous productivity improvements to fund R&D and marketing, aiming for sustainable, balanced top- and bottom-line growth (10-K, 2025-08-04). The franchise is characterized by high competition, significant market share positions, and a customer base led by Walmart (16% of sales) (10-K, 2025-08-04).

**2. Recent Narrative Developments (Dated)**
*   **Corporate & Capital Allocation (2025-08-29):** Analysis indicates a $1,000 investment in PG stock five years ago grew to $1,270, significantly underperforming the S&P 500 index over the same period.
*   **Product/Regulation (2025-08-19):** The National Advertising Division found most performance claims for Crest’s Deep Stain Remover toothpaste supported but recommended modifying or discontinuing an "extra strength fluoride" claim due to FDA monograph limitations.
*   **Operational Outlook (2025-09-19):** Web context notes PG's Q3 2025 results, where a "sales miss" overshadowed earnings, presenting a challenge for its fiscal 2026 outlook which involves tactical and strategic adjustments.
*   **Strategic Positioning (2025-09-29):** News articles consistently position PG as a "Dividend King" and a recession-proof, defensive investment, highlighting its consistent dividend growth history (referencing 67 consecutive years in one snippet).

**3. Material Qualitative Risks and Offsets**
*   **Risk: Litigation & Product Safety.** The company faces ongoing class action lawsuits alleging failure to disclose unsafe levels of lead in Tampax tampons, with potential for broad consumer and legal scrutiny (2025-08-25, 2025-08-19).
*   **Risk: Operational & Regulatory.** A sales miss in Q3 2025 highlights potential execution or demand headwinds (2025-09-19). PG is subject to extensive global regulation (FDA, EPA, etc.) and expanding environmental/sustainability rules, though it does not expect compliance costs to materially impact fiscal 2026 earnings relative to prior years (10-K, 2025-08-04). Minor anti-profiteering ruling in India (2025-09-12).
*   **Risk: Sustainability Ambition.** The company acknowledges that "scalable infrastructure and cost-effective solutions are not yet available" to fully deliver on some of its 2040 net-zero and other environmental ambitions (10-K, 2025-08-04).
*   **Offset: Brand & Market Position.** PG holds leadership or significant market share in competitive segments, supported by strong trademarks, patents, and a integrated strategy focused on product superiority (10-K, 2025-08-04).
*   **Offset: Business Model Resilience.** The company's focus on daily-use consumer staples and its longstanding dividend history underpin its characterization as a stable, defensive business (2025-09-29).

**4. Concise Qualitative Assessment**
**Neutral.** The evidence presents a balanced picture. PG's core franchise as a global leader in consumer staples is robust and defensive, which is a sustained positive. However, recent narrative highlights meaningful operational headwinds (Q3 2025 sales miss), significant ongoing litigation risks related to product safety, and a clear historical pattern of stock underperformance relative to the broader market. The company's strategy for balanced growth is articulated, but its near-term execution is questioned. The combination of a stable but slow-growth core business with active litigation and operational challenges supports a neutral stance.

*Note: All analysis is based strictly on the supplied textual evidence and cited dates. No financial projections or portfolio management advice is inferred or given.*

pm_decision_obj.executive_summary: PG trades at a historically compressed valuation (PE 24.2, near the bottom of its one-year range) with a stable defensive franchise, but recent sequential revenue and earnings declines, ongoing litigation over Tampax product safety, and a single 5-year underperformance data point temper enthusiasm. The risk/reward is roughly balanced with a slight positive tilt from valuation mean-reversion potential.
pm_decision_obj.investment_thesis: PG trades at a historically compressed valuation (PE 24.2, near the bottom of its one-year range) with a stable defensive franchise, but recent sequential revenue and earnings declines, ongoing litigation over Tampax product safety, and a single 5-year underperformance data point temper enthusiasm. The risk/reward is roughly balanced with a slight positive tilt from valuation mean-reversion potential.
key_factors: Valuation compression: PE ratio of 24.155 sits at the lower end of its one-year range (23.7–30.8), with the multiple declining from 28.7 in October 2024; a reversion to the midpoint (~27.3) implies ~13% upside assuming flat earnings.
Weak recent momentum: Q3 2025 (2025-03-31) showed sequential revenue decline of 9.6% ($19.78B vs $21.88B) and EPS decline of 18.1% ($1.54 vs $1.88), though YoY EPS grew modestly at 1.3%.

Balance-sheet resilience: Total equity grew to $52.55B from $48.01B over seven quarters while total liabilities declined from $74.52B to $70.44B, improving the D/E ratio to 1.34 and providing financial stability.

Litigation and regulatory risk: Ongoing class action lawsuits over undisclosed lead levels in Tampax tampons (2025-08-25) and a National Advertising Division recommendation to modify Crest claims (2025-08-19) represent non-trivial headwinds to the brand narrative.

SHW.quantitative_report:
**Quantitative Report: SHW (as of 2025-10-01)**

**1. Financial Trajectory**
- **Revenue**: Q2 2025 revenue was $6.31B, +0.7% YoY and +19.0% QoQ. TTM revenue was $23.08B. The pattern is seasonal with Q2/Q3 > $6B, Q1/Q4 ~$5.3B.
- **Earnings**: Q2 2025 EPS was $3.00, -14.3% YoY and +50.0% QoQ. TTM net income was $2.54B.
- **Margins**: Q2 2025 gross margin was 49.4% ($3.12B/$6.31B), operating margin 15.6% ($985.70M/$6.31B), net margin 11.96% ($754.70M/$6.31B). YoY, gross margin +60 bps, but operating margin -690 bps and net margin -224 bps (vs. Q2 2024).
- **Cash Flow**: Not available in the supplied data.
- **Balance Sheet**: Total equity grew to $4.40B (Q2 2025) from $3.78B (Q3 2023). Total debt declined to $8.98B from $9.60B. Debt-to-equity improved to 2.04 from 2.54.

**2. Earnings Quality & Balance-Sheet Resilience**
- **Earnings Quality**: Net income volatility is high (e.g., Q4 2024 operating loss of -$166.60M but net income of $480.10M). YoY net income fell -15.2% while revenue grew 0.7%, indicating margin pressure. Cash flow data unavailable to assess accruals.
- **Balance-Sheet Resilience**: Debt-to-equity of 2.04 is high but improving (was 2.71 in Q1 2024). Equity has increased 16% since Q3 2023 ($3.78B to $4.40B). The balance sheet is leveraged but trending toward lower leverage.

**3. Valuation vs. History**
- **P/E Diluted**: 34.192, within the 1-year range of 29.7 to 39.7.
- **P/B**: 19.772, within the 1-year range of 18.9 to 26.4.
- **Net Margin**: 0.11, stable within the 0.10–0.11 range over the past year.
- **ROE**: 0.578, down from a 1-year high of 0.67.
- Valuation multiples are mid-range relative to recent history, not indicating extreme cheapness or richness.

**4. Price Context vs. Fundamentals**
- **Price**: $346.26 (2025-09-30), down -9.8% annually and -2.5% quarterly.
- **Range**: 52-week high $400.42, low $308.84.
- **Fundamental Link**: The annual price decline aligns with YoY earnings decline (-14.3% EPS) and flat revenue growth (+0.7%). The stock is trading below its high but above its low, consistent with mixed fundamentals.

**5. Strongest Quantitative Upside & Downside**
- **Upside**: If operating margins recover to Q2 2024 level (22.5%) on stable revenue, EPS could expand significantly. Debt reduction trend could improve equity value.
- **Downside**: Continued margin compression (operating margin fell to -3.1% in Q4 2024) and high leverage (debt/equity 2.04) risk further earnings erosion. Price could retest 52-week low $308.84 on deteriorating fundamentals.

**6. Provisional Quantitative Risk/Reward Conclusion**
Fundamentals show flat revenue, declining earnings, and improving balance sheet. Valuation is mid-range relative to recent history. Risk is high leverage and margin volatility; reward depends on margin recovery. The quantitative signals are balanced, warranting a neutral stance.
SHW.qualitative_report:
**Qualitative Report: Sherwin-Williams (SHW)**
**Date:** 2025-10-01

**1. Business/Franchise Read (from 10-K, 2025-02-20)**
Sherwin-Williams is a global leader in the manufacture, distribution, and sale of paints and coatings. Its business is structured into three reportable segments: **Paint Stores Group** (4,773 company-operated stores in the U.S., Canada, and Caribbean serving contractors and DIY customers), **Consumer Brands Group** (manufacturing for retailers and own stores in Latin America, with ~63% of sales as intersegment transfers), and **Performance Coatings Group** (industrial coatings for global markets). The company emphasizes a strong portfolio of owned and licensed brands (e.g., Sherwin-Williams®, Valspar®, Krylon®, Minwax®) and a vast distribution network. Competition is described as extensive across local, regional, national, and international players, with factors like product quality, innovation, and service driving advantage.

**2. Recent Developments (from supplied text, dated Aug-Sep 2025)**
*   **Growth Catalyst (2025-08-17):** The Suvinil acquisition in Brazil is identified as a key catalyst, expected to close in late 2025, providing access to a high-margin growth market with a projected 4.5% CAGR through 2030.
*   **Capital Allocation & Guidance (2025-09-10):** The company announced a repurchase of 1.45 million shares in Q2 2025, interpreted as a signal of confidence in long-term value. This action was noted alongside the context of "softer earnings guidance."
*   **Valuation Narrative (2025-09-28):** The prevailing analyst narrative suggests the stock is undervalued by nearly 10% based on long-term earnings potential and forecasted growth, citing a discount rate of 7.6%.
*   **Shareholder Returns (2025-08-23):** The company was highlighted as a top dividend stock in its sector (Materials) for the remainder of 2025, noted for dividend performance, growth potential, and market positioning.

**3. Material Qualitative Risks and Offsets**
*   **Risks:**
    *   **Macroeconomic Sensitivity & Competition:** The 10-K (2025-02-20) details risks from economic downturns, inflation, interest rates, and intense competition that can pressure margins and demand. The September 10, 2025, note on "softer guidance and flat sales" aligns with this cyclical risk.
    *   **Input Cost Volatility:** The business section confirms raw materials, particularly petrochemical derivatives, are a major cost. Price volatility and sourcing disruptions are explicit risks.
    *   **Integration & Expansion Execution:** The Suvinil acquisition (noted 2025-08-17) carries inherent integration risks and is a key bet on growth in a new geographic market.
    *   **Litigation:** The 10-K cautions about risks related to "lead pigment and lead-based paint litigation" and environmental remediation liabilities, which are long-standing franchise overhangs.
    *   **DIY Demand Headwinds:** Referenced in the 2025-08-17 article as a current challenge, potentially linked to macroeconomic factors.
*   **Offsets:**
    *   **Scale & Brand Portfolio:** The 10-K describes a dominant U.S. store network and a powerful portfolio of recognized brands across multiple segments and geographies, providing pricing power and customer stickiness.
    *   **Active Capital Return:** The 2025-09-10 share buyback demonstrates management's ability and willingness to return capital, which can support shareholder returns amid softer growth.
    *   **Strategic Growth Avenues:** The Suvinil acquisition (2025-08-17) and the ongoing build-out of global capabilities (noted in the Consumer Brands Group description) are proactive steps to find new growth levers.
    *   **Favorable Valuation View:** The market narrative on 2025-09-28 suggests underlying earnings power may not be fully reflected in the share price.

**4. Concise Qualitative Assessment**
**Neutral-to-Cautiously Positive.** The Sherwin-Williams franchise is fundamentally strong, with a leading market position, iconic brands, and a scaled distribution model. Recent developments show management actively pursuing growth (Brazil acquisition) and rewarding shareholders (buybacks). However, the qualitative narrative is tempered by acknowledged near-term "softer guidance" and persistent macroeconomic and competitive risks. The overall picture is one of a resilient, high-quality business navigating a challenging cyclical environment, with long-term potential supported by strategic actions but subject to execution and market risks.

corrections.issue: The qualitative report states the Suvinil acquisition is 'expected to close in late 2025' and describes it as a 'high-margin growth market with a projected 4.5% CAGR through 2030.' This is sourced from a single ainvest.com article (2025-08-17). The 10-K business section does not mention Suvinil at all, and no other supplied evidence corroborates the 4.5% CAGR figure or the 'high-margin' characterization. The PM should treat these specific claims as analyst-sourced rather than company-confirmed.

However, the risk search results (SHW risks lawsuit regulation) returned no relevant results about SHW-specific litigation — all five results were generic legal articles unrelated to Sherwin-Williams. The qualitative report correctly sourced this from the 10-K, but the absence of any recent SHW-specific litigation news in the supplied evidence should be noted as a gap rather than a current risk escalation.
corrections.required_action: Note that no SHW-specific litigation developments appear in the recent news evidence; the litigation risk is sourced solely from the 10-K boilerplate cautionary language.

The Q2 2024 operating income was $1.41B on $6.27B revenue, yielding ~22.5% operating margin. The delta is indeed ~690 bps. However, the report also states 'net margin -224 bps (vs. Q2 2024).' Q2 2024 net margin was 889.9/6270 = 14.2%, Q2 2025 was 754.7/6310 = 11.96%, a delta of ~224 bps. These calculations are consistent with the raw data. No issue here — verified.
corrections.required_action: No action needed; verified correct.

unsupported_claims: The qualitative report's claim that the Suvinil acquisition provides access to a market 'with a projected 4.5% CAGR through 2030' and is a 'high-margin growth avenue' is sourced only from a single third-party article (ainvest.com, 2025-08-17) and is not corroborated by any company filing or other supplied evidence.

The qualitative report's statement that 'the prevailing analyst narrative suggests the stock is undervalued by nearly 10% based on long-term earnings potential and forecasted growth, citing a discount rate of 7.6%' is sourced from a simplywall.st article (2025-09-28) — this is a third-party valuation model, not a company or fundamental data point. The quantitative report's own valuation assessment (P/E 34.2, mid-range) does not support a strong undervaluation claim.

The Suvinil acquisition in Brazil (expected late 2025) is a potential growth catalyst, but the 4.5% CAGR and high-margin claims originate from a single third-party source and are not corroborated by company filings, tempering conviction on this thesis.

  filing_date=2025-02-27 section=business period_end=2024-12-31
ITEM  1.    BUSINESS
OUR BUSINESSES
Overview
The terms "we," "our," "us," "its," "UnitedHealth Group," or the "Company" used in this report refer to UnitedHealth Group Incorporated and its subsidiaries.
UnitedHealth Group Incorporated is a
…[truncated 21778 chars]…
ate and international governments continue to consider and enact various legislative and regulatory proposals which could materially impact certain aspects of the health care system. New laws, regulations and rules, or changes in the interpretation of existing laws, regulations and rules, including as a result of changes in the political environment, could adversely affect our businesses.
See Part I, Item 1A, "Risk Factors" for a discussion of the risks related to our compliance with U.S. federal and state and international laws and regulations.
U.S. Federal Laws and Regulation
When we contract with the federal government, we are subject to federal laws and regulations relating to the award, administration and performance of U.S. government contracts. CMS regulates our UnitedHealthcare businesses and certain aspects of our Optum businesses. Payments by CMS to our businesses are subject to regulations, including those governing fee-for-service and the submission of information relating to the health status of enrollees for purposes of determining the amounts of certain payments to us. CMS also has the right to audit our performance to determine our compliance with CMS contracts and regulations and the quality of care we provide to Medicare beneficiaries. Our commercial business is further subject to CMS audits related to medical loss ratios (MLRs) and risk adjustment data.
UnitedHealthcare Community & State has Medicaid and CHIP contracts, which are subject to federal regulations regarding services to be provided to Medicaid enrollees, payment for those services and other aspects of these programs. There are many regulations affecting Medicare and Medicaid compliance, and the regulatory environment with respect to these programs is complex.
Our businesses are also subject to laws and regulations relating to consumer protection, anti-fraud and abuse, anti-kickbacks, false claims, prohibited referrals, inappropriate reduction or limitation of health care services, anti-money laundering and securities and antitrust compliance.
Privacy, Security and Data Standards Regulation.
Certain of our operations are subject to regulation under the administrative simplification provisions of the Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act of 1996, as amended (HIPAA), which apply to both the group and individual health insurance markets, including self-funded employee benefit plans. Federal regulations related to HIPAA contain minimum standards for electronic transactions and code sets and for the privacy and security of protected health information.
Our businesses must comply with the Health Information Technology for Economic and Clinical Health Act (HITECH), which regulates matters relating to privacy, security and data standards. HITECH imposes requirements on uses and disclosures of health information; includes contracting requirements for HIPAA business associate agreements; extends parts of HIPAA privacy and security provisions to business associates; adds federal data breach notification requirements for covered entities and business associates and reporting requirements to HHS and the Federal Trade Commission (FTC) and, in some cases, to the local media; strengthens enforcement and imposes higher financial penalties for HIPAA violations and, in certain cases, imposes criminal penalties for individuals, including employees. In the conduct of our business, depending on the circumstances, we may act as either a covered entity or a business associate.
5
Table of Contents
The use and disclosure of individually identifiable health data by our businesses are also regulated in some instances by other federal laws, including the Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act (GLBA) or state statutes implementing GLBA. These federal laws and state statutes generally require insurers to provide customers with notice regarding how their non-public personal health and financial information is used and the opportunity to "opt out" of certain disclosures before the insurer shares such information with a third party, and generally prescribe safeguards for the protection of personal information. Neither the GLBA nor HIPAA privacy regulations preempt more stringent state laws and regulations, which may apply to us, as discussed below. Federal consumer protection laws may also apply in some instances to privacy and security practices related to personally identifiable information.
ERISA.
The Employee Retirement Income Security Act of 1974, as amended (ERISA), regulates how our services are provided to or through certain types of employer-sponsored health benefit plans. ERISA is a set of laws and regulations subject to interpretation by the U.S. Department of Labor (DOL) as well as the federal courts. ERISA sets forth standards on how our business units may do business with employers who sponsor employee health benefit plans, particularly those who maintain self-funded plans. Regulations established by the DOL subject us to additional requirements for administration of benefits, claims payment and member appeals under health care plans governed by ERISA.
State Laws and Regulation
Health Care Regulation.
Our insurance and HMO subsidiaries must be licensed by the jurisdictions in which they conduct business. All of the states in which our subsidiaries offer insurance and HMO products regulate those products and operations. The states require periodic financial reports and establish minimum capital or restricted cash reserve requirements. The National Association of Insurance Commissioners (NAIC) has adopted model regulations, which require expanded governance practices and risk and solvency assessment reporting. Most states have adopted these or similar measures to expand the scope of regulations relating to corporate governance and internal control activities of HMOs and insurance companies. We are required to maintain a risk management framework and file a confidential self-assessment report with state insurance regulators. We file reports annually with Connecticut, our lead regulator, and with New York, as required by the state's regulation.
Our health plans and insurance companies are regulated under state insurance holding company regulations. Such regulations generally require registration with applicable state departments of insurance and the filing of reports describing capital structure, ownership, financial condition, certain affiliated transactions and general business operations. Most state insurance holding company laws and regulations require prior regulatory approval of acquisitions and material affiliated transfers of assets, as well as transactions between the regulated companies and their parent holding companies or affiliates. These laws may restrict the ability of our regulated subsidiaries to pay dividends to our holding companies.
Some of our business activity is subject to other health care-related regulations and requirements, including PPO, Managed Care Organization (MCO), utilization review (UR), TPA, pharmacy care services, durable medical equipment or care provider-related regulations and licensure requirements. These regulations differ from state to state and may contain network, contracting, product and rate, licensing and financial and reporting requirements. Health care-related laws and regulations set specific standards for delivery of services, appeals, grievances and payment of claims, adequacy of health care professional networks, fraud prevention, protection of consumer health information, pricing and underwriting practices and covered benefits and services. State health care anti-fraud and abuse prohibitions encompass a wide range of activities, including kickbacks for referral of members, billing for unnecessary medical services and improper marketing. UNH.quantitative_report:
## UNH Quantitative Report — Decision Date 2025-10-01

### 1. Revenue, Earnings, Margin, Cash-Flow & Balance-Sheet Trajectory

**Revenue:** TTM revenue stands at $422.82B. Quarterly revenue has grown steadily from $92.36B (Q3 2023) to $111.62B (Q2 2025), a cumulative +20.8% over eight quarters. YoY revenue growth in the latest quarter is +12.9% ($111.62B vs. $98.86B). QoQ growth slowed to +1.9%.

**Earnings:** TTM net income is $22.09B. However, the latest quarter (Q2 2025) reported EPS of $3.74, a sharp -45.4% QoQ decline from $6.85 in Q1 2025 and -17.6% YoY versus Q2 2024's $4.54. Q1 2024 was an anomaly at -$1.53 EPS. Excluding that outlier, Q2 2025 EPS is the lowest in the eight-quarter window.

**Margins:** Gross margin is robust at 88.2% (latest). Operating margin compressed sharply to 4.6% in Q2 2025 ($5.15B operating income on $111.62B revenue) versus 8.3% in Q1 2025 ($9.12B on $109.58B). Net margin fell to 3.2% in Q2 2025 versus 5.9% in Q1 2025. TTM operating income is $30.75B.

**Cash flow:** No cash-flow data is supplied across the entire eight-quarter window. This is a material evidence gap.

**Balance sheet:** Total assets grew from $282.06B (Q3 2023) to $308.57B (Q2 2025), +9.4%. Total equity expanded from $89.56B to $100.47B, +12.2%. The debt-to-equity ratio (total liabilities/equity) stands at 2.03x as of Q2 2025 ($203.79B / $100.47B), roughly stable versus 2.10x a year earlier ($186.82B / $94.68B). Equity growth is modestly outpacing liability growth.

---

### 2. Earnings Quality & Balance-Sheet Resilience

**Earnings quality — concerns:** The Q2 2025 quarter shows a severe disconnect: revenue grew +12.9% YoY while net income fell -19.2% YoY. Operating income of $5.15B is down -34.6% QoQ ($7.88B in Q2 2024). The cost-of-revenue line rose +14.8% YoY ($13.02B vs. $11.34B), but operating expenses beyond COGS appear to have surged, compressing operating margin from 8.0% (Q2 2024) to 4.6%. Net margin of 3.2% is the lowest non-negative quarter in the dataset. Without cash-flow statements, the cash conversion of reported earnings cannot be verified.

**Balance-sheet resilience — adequate but leveraged:** The equity base of $100.47B provides a meaningful cushion. Assets-to-equity ratio is 3.07x, indicating moderate leverage. Quarterly equity has been positive and growing every period since Q3 2023's $89.56B. However, the $203.79B in total liabilities is roughly 2x equity — acceptable for a managed-care/health-services firm with float-like liabilities, but it limits margin for error if the margin compression persists.

---

### 3. Valuation Versus Supplied History

| Metric | Latest (2025-09-30) | 1-Year High | 1-Year Low |
|---|---|---|---|
| P/E diluted | 14.2x | 38.7x | 9.5x |
| P/B | 3.13x | 5.9x | 2.2x |
| P/S | 0.74x | n/a | n/a |
| Net margin | 5.2% | n/a | n/a |
| ROE | 22.0% | n/a | n/a |

The current P/E of 14.2x sits near the bottom of the 9.5–38.7x range, but this must be contextualized: the "E" denominator in Q2 2025 is depressed ($3.74 EPS vs. a more typical ~$6+). If Q2's margin compression is transient, the normalized P/E on ~$24+ TTM EPS (annualizing Q1 2025's $6.85 rather than Q2's $3.74) would be higher, closer to ~21x. If the Q2 margin contraction is structural, 14.2x may still not be cheap. P/B of 3.13x is also at the lower end of 2.2–5.9x, correlating with the lower ROE (22.0% vs. 15.8% a year earlier — actually an improvement, driven by the denominator shift in net income composition across quarters). The sparse valuation history shows P/E compressed from 36.3x in October 2024 to 11.3x in July 2025 before a partial rebound to 14.2x.

---

### 4. Price Level & Return Context Versus Fundamentals

**Price:** $345.30 on 2025-09-30. The 52-week range is $234.60–$630.73; the stock sits at the 29th percentile of that range.

**Returns:** Quarterly return (Q3 2025) is +5.9% ($326.14 → $345.30). The prior quarter (Q2 2025) was -37.7% ($523.75 → $326.14) — a catastrophic drawdown. The annual return is -39.9% ($574.81 → $345.30).

**Fundamental-price linkage:** The stock's ~40% annual decline coincides with the earnings deterioration visible from Q1 2025 onward (EPS: $6.85 → $3.74). The P/E compression from ~32x to ~14x reflects both price decline and earnings normalization. The Q2 2025 margin collapse — operating margin dropping from ~8% to ~4.6% — is the primary fundamental catalyst for the re-rating. The modest Q3 2025 price recovery (+5.9%) has not reversed the structural damage.

---

### 5. Strongest Quantitative Upside and Downside

**Upside:**
- Revenue momentum remains strong at +12.9% YoY, with gross margins holding at ~88%. If operating expenses in Q2 2025 are a one-time event (e.g., integration costs, litigation reserve), and operating margins revert to the 7–8% range seen in Q3 2024 through Q1 2025, TTM operating income could recover toward $32–34B, supporting materially higher earnings and a re-rating toward the historical P/E range of 25–35x.
- The current P/E of 14.2x is at a multi-year trough; mean-reversion to even the midpoint of the supplied range (~24x) implies significant re-rating potential.
- ROE of 22.0% remains robust, supporting the equity base.

**Downside:**
- Q2 2025's operating margin of 4.6% could persist or worsen if cost pressures are structural. A sustained 4.6% operating margin on ~$111B quarterly revenue yields ~$5.1B/quarter or ~$20B annualized operating income — roughly flat to TTM but with recent quarterly deterioration.
- If the Q2 EPS run-rate of $3.74 is the new baseline, annualized EPS would be ~$15, implying the current P/E is actually ~23x — no longer cheap.
- Net income volatility is extreme: -$1.53 EPS (Q1 2024), then $4.54, $6.51, $5.98, $6.85, and now $3.74. This pattern suggests earnings durability is uncertain.
- The -39.9% annual drawdown signals potential institutional de-rating; further negative revisions could extend the decline.

---

### 6. Provisional Quantitative Risk/Reward Conclusion

The evidence presents a classic value trap versus mean-reversion dilemma. Revenue trajectory is unambiguously strong (+12.9% YoY, ~$423B TTM), and the balance sheet is resilient ($100.5B equity, stable leverage). However, the Q2 2025 operating margin collapse to 4.6% — from 8.3% just one quarter prior — is a severe negative signal whose permanence cannot be assessed from the numbers alone. The current P/E of 14.2x is near cycle troughs, but if Q2's depressed earnings are representative rather than anomalous, the "true" multiple is materially higher.

**Quantitative risk/reward skews moderately positive** contingent on one critical assumption: that Q2 2025's margin compression is non-recurring. Under that assumption, the -39.9% annual drawdown against +12.9% revenue growth creates a significant disconnect. Without cash-flow data and without visibility into whether the margin deterioration is structural, the position carries meaningful uncertainty. The missing cash-flow evidence is a material analytical blind spot.
UNH.qualitative_report:
### Qualitative Report: UnitedHealth Group (UNH)  
**Decision Date:** 2025-10-01  
**Evidence Cutoff:** Point-in-time before 2025-10-01  

---

#### 1. Business/Franchise Read  
UnitedHealth Group is a health care and well-being company operating through two complementary businesses: **UnitedHealthcare** (health benefits) and **Optum** (health services). UnitedHealthcare offers a range of health benefits plans across Employer & Individual (29.7 million members), Medicare & Retirement (Medicare Advantage: 7.8 million; Medicare Part D: 10.1 million; Medicare Supplement: 4.3 million), and Community & State (7.4 million members). Premium revenues from CMS (Medicare) represented 40% of total consolidated revenues in 2024. Optum provides technology-enabled health services through Optum Health (serving 100 million consumers), Optum Insight (backlog: $32.8 billion), and Optum Rx (managing $178 billion in pharmaceutical spending in 2024). The franchise leverages data analytics, integrated care delivery, and scale to address affordability, access, and outcomes across the health system.  

#### 2. Recent Developments (Dated)  
**Corporate Actions & Capital Allocation:**  
- **2025-09-12:** UnitedHealth Group’s stock gained 24.2% in August, rebounding from a July decline, driven by Berkshire Hathaway’s investment and the closing of the Amedisys acquisition.  
- **2025-08-27:** Regulatory actions included a $528 million Amedisys divestiture.  
- **2025-08-19:** Warren Buffett and other investors invested over $1.6 billion in UNH amid a 2025 stock decline, targeting Medicare Advantage growth and regulatory resolution.  

**Regulatory & Legal:**  
- **2025-09-19:** UnitedHealth Group may pivot its growth strategy away from aggressive vertical acquisitions toward organic innovation in care delivery or expansion into less scrutinized areas, due to regulatory pressures.  
- **2025-08-27:** The company faces DOJ antitrust lawsuits, Medicare Advantage fraud probes, and congressional scrutiny, posing threats to its integrated model.  

**Earnings & Guidance:**  
- **2025-09-08:** Barclays raised UNH’s price target to $352, citing long-term growth potential despite Q2 2025 earnings misses driven by $6.5 billion in unexpected medical costs.  
- **2025-09-30:** UnitedHealthcare provided guidance for Medicare’s Annual Enrollment Period, highlighting plan options for seniors.  

**Product & Partnerships:**  
- **2025-09-25:** Plus Therapeutics secured a national coverage agreement with UnitedHealthcare for a CNS cancer diagnostic test, covering over 51 million people.  

**Market Sentiment:**  
- **2025-09-13:** UNH was a top-performing S&P 500 stock in August 2025, with a 24.2% monthly gain.  
- **2025-09-30:** Analysts note UNH’s potential recovery driven by demographic trends, cost-cutting, and AI integration despite ongoing challenges.  

#### 3. Material Qualitative Risks & Offsets  
**Risks:**  
- **Regulatory & Legal Exposure:** DOJ antitrust investigations, Medicare fraud probes, congressional scrutiny, and cybersecurity lawsuits (e.g., Nebraska’s suit) could lead to fines, divestitures, or operational constraints (2025-08-27, 2025-08-14).  
- **Operational & Financial:** Cybersecurity failures pose existential risks (2025-08-14). Medicare cost overruns (e.g., $6.5B unexpected costs in Q2 2025) and potential Medicare Advantage rate cuts pressure margins (2025-09-08).  
- **Strategic:** Shift away from vertical acquisitions may limit growth avenues (2025-09-19).  

**Offsets:**  
- **Scale & Integration:** Dominant market position with integrated Optum-UnitedHealthcare model enhances data analytics and care coordination.  
- **Demographic Tailwinds:** Aging population supports Medicare Advantage growth (2025-08-19).  
- **Investor Confidence:** Significant investments by Berkshire Hathaway and others signal long-term conviction (2025-09-12, 2025-08-19).  
- **Strategic Adjustments:** Cost-cutting plans, AI integration, and a potential pivot to organic growth initiatives aim to improve margins (2025-09-30, 2025-09-19).  

#### 4. Qualitative Assessment  
**Neutral to Slightly Positive.** UnitedHealth Group maintains a robust franchise with integrated health care capabilities and demographic tailwinds. However, material regulatory/legal headwinds, recent earnings volatility, and operational risks introduce significant uncertainty. Positive catalysts include strategic adjustments, investor backing, and recent market recovery, but the outcome of ongoing probes and cost pressures remains a key determinant of near-term performance.

corrections.issue: Quant report states 'ROE of 22.0% vs. 15.8% a year earlier — actually an improvement, driven by the denominator shift in net income composition across quarters.' The raw evidence shows ROE=0.158 (15.8%) at 2024-10-01 and ROE=0.22 (22.0%) at 2025-09-30, so the numbers are correct. However, the parenthetical explanation 'driven by the denominator shift in net income composition across quarters' is vague and potentially misleading — the ROE improvement is primarily driven by the TTM net income numerator rising (from the Q1 2024 negative quarter rolling off and being replaced by higher-earnings quarters), not a 'denominator shift.' This is a minor interpretive issue but could confuse the PM.
corrections.required_action: No action required; note for PM that the ROE improvement is driven by the TTM net income numerator (negative Q1 2024 EPS rolling off), not a balance-sheet denominator effect.

corrections.issue: Qual report states 'Medicare cost overruns (e.g., $6.5B unexpected costs in Q2 2025)' citing 2025-09-08. The raw qualitative evidence confirms this from the Barclays article. However, the quant report attributes the Q2 2025 margin compression to operating expenses surging without identifying the $6.5B medical cost driver. The two reports are not contradictory, but the quant report's silence on the specific $6.5B cause (which appears in the qual pack) means the quant analysis treats the margin collapse as unexplained when the qual pack provides a concrete explanation.
corrections.required_action: PM should note that the qual report's $6.5B unexpected medical cost (from Barclays/2025-09-08) provides a specific driver for the quant report's observed Q2 2025 margin compression, which the quant report flags but cannot explain from numbers alone.

corrections.issue: Quant report states 'P/E compression from ~32x to ~14x' and 'P/E of 14.2x sits near the bottom of the 9.5–38.7x range.' The raw evidence shows the P/E trough was 11.291 on 2025-07-28, not 9.5x. The 9.5x figure appears in the range metadata but is not directly visible in the 12-point sparse history. The quant report's characterization of 14.2x as 'near the bottom' is slightly misleading — it is above the 11.3x trough by ~26%. The report does acknowledge the 11.3x figure in the sparse history discussion, so this is internally noted but the headline framing could overstate the current discount.
corrections.required_action: No action required; the 14.2x is above the 11.3x trough (Jul 2025), so the 'near cycle troughs' framing is directionally correct but the PM should be aware the stock already bounced from a lower P/E level.

unsupported_claims: > Quant report: 'ROE of 22.0% vs. 15.8% a year earlier — actually an improvement, driven by the denominator shift in net income composition across quarters.' The raw evidence confirms both ROE figures (0.158 at 2024-10-01, 0.22 at 2025-09-30), but the phrase 'denominator shift in net income composition across quarters' is not supported by the evidence and is misleading — ROE improvement is driven by the TTM net income numerator rising as the negative Q1 2024 quarter rolls off.
verified_points

pm_decision_obj.executive_summary: UNH presents a classic value trap vs. mean-reversion dilemma: revenue growth remains robust at +12.9% YoY and the stock trades at a trough P/E of 14.2x after a -40% drawdown, but Q2 2025's operating margin collapse to 4.6% (from 8.3% prior quarter) driven by $6.5B in unexpected medical costs creates material uncertainty about earnings durability. Berkshire Hathaway's $1.6B investment and strong demographic tailwinds provide a floor, but regulatory overhang and the need to confirm margin recovery warrant a cautious Hold.
pm_decision_obj.investment_thesis: UNH presents a classic value trap vs. mean-reversion dilemma: revenue growth remains robust at +12.9% YoY and the stock trades at a trough P/E of 14.2x after a -40% drawdown, but Q2 2025's operating margin collapse to 4.6% (from 8.3% prior quarter) driven by $6.5B in unexpected medical costs creates material uncertainty about earnings durability. Berkshire Hathaway's $1.6B investment and strong demographic tailwinds provide a floor, but regulatory overhang and the need to confirm margin recovery warrant a cautious Hold.
key_factors: Revenue momentum remains strong at +12.9% YoY ($111.62B Q2 2025 vs. $98.86B Q2 2024), with TTM revenue of $422.8B and gross margins holding at 88.2%, demonstrating the franchise's fundamental demand.

Q2 2025 operating margin collapsed to 4.6% from 8.3% in Q1 2025, driven by $6.5B in unexpected medical costs (per Barclays, 2025-09-08); whether this is transient or structural is the central question for the stock's re-rating.

P/E of 14.2x sits near cycle troughs (vs. 1-year range of 9.5-38.7x), but if Q2's depressed $3.74 EPS is the new baseline rather than an anomaly, the normalized P/E is ~23x and the discount is illusory.

Berkshire Hathaway and other investors committed $1.6B+ in August 2025 (2025-08-19), signaling institutional conviction in long-term Medicare Advantage growth, while the stock rebounded 24.2% in August from its July lows.

Latest 10-K business s

nd fulfillment services, health and wellness, and financial services to provide our customers a broader set of offerings to meet expanding needs.
Seasonal Aspects of Operations.
Our business is seasonal to a certain extent and varies by country due to different national and religious holidays, festivals and customs, as well as different weather patterns. Historically, our highest sales volume for each segment has occurred in the fourth quarter of our fiscal year.
Suppliers and Supply Chain.
As a retailer and warehouse club operator, we utilize a global supply chain that includes both U.S. and international suppliers from whom we purchase the merchandise that we sell in our stores, clubs and online. In many instances, we purchase merchandise from producers located near the stores and clubs in which such merchandise will be sold, particularly products in the "fresh" category. Consistent with applicable laws, we offer our suppliers the opportunity to efficiently sell significant quantities of their products to us. These relationships enable us to obtain pricing that reflects the volume, certainty and cost-effectiveness these arrangements provide to such suppliers, which in turn enables us to provide low prices to our customers. Our suppliers are subject to standards of conduct, including requirements that they comply with local labor laws, local worker safety laws and other applicable laws. Our ability to acquire from our suppliers the assortment and volume of products we wish to offer to our customers, to receive those products within the required time through our supply chain and to distribute those products to our stores and clubs, determines, along with other supply chain logistics matters (such as containers or port access for example), in part, our in-stock levels in our stores and clubs and the attractiveness of our merchandise assortment we offer to our customers and members.
Intellectual Property.
We regard our trademarks, service marks, copyrights, patents, domain names, trade dress, trade secrets, proprietary technologies and similar intellectual property as important to our success, and with respect to our associates, customers and others, we rely on trademark, copyright, and patent laws, trade-secret protection, and confidentiality and/or license agreements to protect our proprietary rights. We have registered, or applied for the registration of, a number of U.S. and international domain names, trademarks, service marks and copyrights. Additionally, we have filed U.S. and international patent applications covering certain of our proprietary technology. We have licensed in the past, and expect that we may license in the future, certain of our proprietary rights to third parties.
Government Regulation.
As a company with global operations, we are subject to the laws of the United States and multiple foreign jurisdictions in which we operate and the rules and regulations of various governing bodies, which may differ among jurisdictions. For additional information, see the risk factors herein in "Item 1A. Risk Factors" under the sub-caption "Legal, Tax, Regulatory, Compliance, Reputational and Other Risks."
10
Our Shared Value Priorities
We seek to operate our business in a way that creates shared value: We believe we maximize long-term value and create competitive advantage for the Company by delivering for our stakeholders, including our customers, associates, shareholders, suppliers, business partners and communities. We believe that addressing their needs builds the value of our business, including by enhancing trust, creating new business opportunities, managing cost and risk, building capabilities for future advantage and strengthening the underlying systems on which we rely.
We prioritize issues that offer the greatest potential for Walmart to create long-term shared value: Issues that rank high in relevance to our business, importance to building and maintaining stakeholder trust and in which Walmart has the ability to be an effective actor. Our current priorities are categorized into four broad themes: opportunity, sustainability, community and ethics and integrity.
- Opportunity. We believe that promoting economic opportunity helps Walmart attract and retain the talent we need to run our business, respond to customer needs, and promote economic resilience in the places where we operate. Our approach includes creating opportunities for Walmart associates (as further described in the Human Capital Management section below), our suppliers, workers in supply chains and the communities we serve. We offer good entry-level jobs in addition to multiple career pathways; endeavor to cultivate a culture of belonging—rooted in respect for the individual—where associates and customers feel seen, supported and connected; and provide tens of thousands of local, national and global suppliers the opportunity to grow with us.
- Sustainability. Walmart's sustainability efforts endeavor to create and preserve long-term business value through increasing the efficiency and resilience of our operations, promoting surety of supply, sourcing responsibly and enhancing our product assortment. With respect to people, we aim to respect the dignity of people throughout our supply chain, create economic opportunity for people working in supply chains, and sell safer, healthier products. With respect to the environment, our efforts aim to enhance the sustainability of product supply chains; preserve, protect, and restore nature; reduce waste; procure renewable sources of energy; and reduce greenhouse gas emissions.
- Community. We believe that Walmart thrives in strong, resilient communities, and we endeavor to help communities thrive. We aim to strengthen communities by providing convenient access to affordable, quality goods and services through our omni-channel retail model and everyday low prices; contributing to the economic vitality of communities by providing quality jobs, training and career paths; investing in local suppliers; and supporting organizations and causes that matter to our customers and associates.
- Ethics and Integrity. We believe that people want to work for and do business with a company they can trust, and Walmart works to create a culture that inspires trust with our stakeholders. We seek to achieve this, by focusing on promoting ethics and compliance, governance and oversight, responsibly engaging in public policy activities, using data and technology responsibly and respecting human rights.
We periodically publish information on our shared value priorities, strategies, progress, and challenges on the environmental, social and governance ("ESG") reporting section of our corporate website and may update those disclosures from time to time. Nothing on our website, including our ESG reporting, documents or sections thereof, shall be deemed incorporated by reference into this Annual Report on Form 10-K or incorporated by reference into any of our other filings with the SEC.
Human Capital Management
Our associates – powered by technology – play a critical role in delivering on our purpose to help people save money and live better. Our business is focused on serving people and this is delivered by our approximately 2.1 million associates around the world with approximately 1.6 million associates in the U.S. and approximately 0.5 million associates internationally. In the U.S., approximately 92% of our associates are hourly and approximately 68% of our associates are full-time.
We believe our people make the difference, and we are focused on investing in the growth and well-being of our associates, investing in digital experiences to improve their quality of work and creating a culture of belonging. An important part of our focus is to provide opportunities for associates to grow and learn. For some, we are a foundational entry point to develop critical skills that are relevant for a variety of caree

WMT.quantitative_report:
**Quantitative Equity Report: WMT**  
*Evidence as of 2025-10-01*

### 1. Financial Trajectory
- **Revenue**: Q2 2025 (July 31) revenue was $177.40B, a +7.1% increase from Q1’s $165.61B. TTM revenue stands at $693.15B.
- **Earnings**: Q2 2025 diluted EPS was $0.88, a +57.1% QoQ increase from Q1’s $0.56. TTM net income was $21.93B. Note historical volatility: Q4 2024 (Jan 31) EPS was -$1.80.
- **Margins**: Q2 2025 gross margin was 25.1% ($44.63B / $177.40B), up QoQ from Q1’s 24.9%. Operating margin was 4.1% ($7.29B / $177.40B). Net margin for the period was 4.0% ($7.15B / $177.40B).
- **Cash Flow**: **No cash flow statement data supplied for any period.**
- **Balance Sheet**: Total equity grew to $96.55B in Q2 2025 from $90.34B in Q1. Total assets were $270.84B. Last reported total debt was $36.00B (as of 2025-01-31).

### 2. Earnings Quality & Balance-Sheet Resilience
- **Earnings Quality**: The QoQ earnings growth (+57.1%) outpaced revenue growth (+7.1%). Net margin of 2.8% is low for the trailing period. Historical EPS includes negative quarters (e.g., -1.80), indicating volatility or potential one-time charges. **Free cash flow data is unavailable**, a key gap for assessing earnings quality.
- **Balance-Sheet Resilience**: Equity has trended upward ($85.58B in 2023-10-31 to $96.55B). Using last available debt ($36.00B) and current equity, a calculated debt-to-equity ratio is 0.37x. Total liabilities to equity was 1.69x as of 2025-01-31 ($163.40B / $97.42B). Leverage appears moderate.

### 3. Valuation Versus History
- **Latest Ratios (2025-09-30)**: P/E diluted = 42.58, P/B = 9.18, P/S = 1.21, net margin = 2.8%, ROE = 21.6%.
- **Historical Context**: The P/E of 42.58 is at the top of its 365-day range (28.8 to 43.1). P/B of 9.18 is also near its high (6.8 to 9.3). The stock is trading at a significant premium to its own recent valuation history. ROE of 21.6% is within its observed range (20.7% to 25.2%).

### 4. Price Level & Return Context
- **Price Action**: On 2025-09-30, WMT closed at $103.06. The 52-week range is $78.98 to $106.11.
- **Returns**: Annual (252-day) return was +29.0%. Quarterly (63-day) return was +4.9%.
- **Fundamental vs. Price Path**: The strong annual price appreciation (+29.0%) occurred alongside a mixed earnings history (including a negative quarter) and current high valuations (P/E at 42.6x). The recent quarterly price gain (+4.9%) aligns with the reported QoQ earnings recovery.

### 5. Strongest Quantitative Upside & Downside
- **Upside**: Demonstrated revenue growth momentum (+7.1% QoQ) and a sharp QoQ earnings rebound (+57.1%). Improving gross margin trajectory (from 24.9% to 25.1% QoQ).
- **Downside**: Valuation is stretched at the top of its historical range (P/E 42.6x vs. 28.8-43.1 range). Absence of free cash flow data limits assessment of cash generation durability. Low net margin (2.8%) and historical earnings volatility (e.g., -$1.80 EPS quarter) present fundamental risk.

### 6. Provisional Quantitative Risk/Reward Conclusion
The quantitative profile presents a **cautious risk/reward**. Positive recent momentum in revenue and earnings is offset by a valuation (P/E 42.6x, P/B 9.18x) that is elevated relative to its own history, low profit margins, and the unavailability of cash flow data to verify earnings quality. The stock’s price has significantly appreciated over the past year (+29.0%), compressing future return potential based on current fundamentals. The balance sheet appears resilient, but the lack of cash flow visibility and stretched multiples suggest limited quantitative margin of safety.
WMT.qualitative_report:
**Qualitative Analysis: WMT (as of 2025-10-01)**

**1. Business/Franchise Read**
Walmart is a global, technology-powered omni-channel retailer operating through three segments: Walmart U.S. (69% of FY2025 net sales), Walmart International (18%), and Sam's Club U.S. (13%). Its core strategy is built on "Everyday Low Price" (EDLP) and "Everyday Low Cost" (EDLC), aiming to be a price leader for busy families. The company is evolving into a broader ecosystem centered on its physical stores and e-commerce, with growing mutually reinforcing businesses in advertising, marketplace fulfillment, and financial services (e.g., PhonePe in India). It emphasizes integrated "omni-channel" shopping via store pickup/delivery, its Walmart+ membership, and technological investments in supply chain automation and AI.

**2. Recent Developments (Dated)**
*   **Guidance & Operations:** On 2025-08-21, Walmart raised its full-year earnings and sales outlook. Concurrently (2025-08-22), it reported 25% e-commerce growth. Its CFO noted (2025-08-22) the company is choosing to absorb some tariff costs. For FY2026, the company expects total net sales growth of 3.75% to 4.75% (2025-09-01).
*   **Growth Catalysts:** Recent commentary highlights drivers including tech-forward "Store of the Future" upgrades, expansion of its advertising arm, warehouse automation, and AI-driven e-commerce enhancements (2025-09-01). Investment in e-commerce and AI is a priority to attract top tech talent (2025-08-22). The company is also seen as a candidate to reach a trillion-dollar valuation (2025-09-16).
*   **Corporate Actions:** Walmart sold its equity investment in JD.com in August 2024, a strategic action noted in its 10-K (2025-03-14) to reshape its international portfolio.
*   **External Environment:** Over 100 Fortune 500 CEOs criticized U.S. tariff policies (2025-09-22). A Numerator report (2025-09-16) indicated U.S. household spending growth is sharply decelerating, with Hispanic consumers becoming more cautious, prioritizing essentials.

**3. Material Qualitative Risks & Offsets**
*   **Risk: Cost Pressures & Competitive Intensity.** Rising tariff costs are a headwind (2025-08-21, 2025-09-22). The retail landscape is described as "highly competitive and rapidly evolving" (10-K), with well-funded competitors in e-commerce and services.
*   **Risk: Legal and Regulatory.** The company faces multiple lawsuits, including shareholder suits over governance and disclosure (2025-08-27), and class actions alleging deceptive pricing (2025-08-27). It recently settled a consumer protection lawsuit for $5.5M (2025-08-13). Marketplace growth brings additional legal risks (2025-09-22).
*   **Risk: Consumer Sentiment & Operational Challenges.** Evidence points to decelerating consumer spending and increased financial caution (2025-09-16). Internal reports mention controversial labor practices regarding safety reporting (2025-08-31).
*   **Offset: Pricing Power & Scale.** The foundational EDLP strategy and massive scale (10,750+ stores, ~$681B in revenue) provide a durable competitive advantage. The company's commitment to absorb some tariff costs reinforces price leadership.
*   **Offset: Digital & Ecosystem Momentum.** Robust e-commerce growth (25% cited) and investments in AI, automation, and high-margin ancillary businesses (advertising, marketplace, financial services) are clear strategic offsets to retail margin pressures.
*   **Offset: Portfolio Management.** Active strategic actions (e.g., exiting certain African operations, selling JD.com stake) aim to sharpen focus on sustainable, profitable markets (10-K).

**4. Qualitative Assessment**
**Positive.** The qualitative narrative is one of a dominant, low-price retailer successfully leveraging its physical footprint and scale to build a powerful digital and ecosystem business. Recent guidance raises and consistent e-commerce growth indicate strong operational momentum. While material risks exist from tariffs, legal exposure, and a potential consumer slowdown, the company's strategic investments and core pricing advantages provide significant offsets. The franchise appears to be strengthening its moat through technological adaptation and service expansion.

***Evidence Separation:*** *All developments and risks are directly cited from the supplied news articles, web context, or 10-K filing with their respective dates. The assessment synthesizes these confirmed evidence points.*

corrections.issue: Quant report states 'Net margin for the period was 4.0% ($7.15B / $177.40B)' for Q2 2025, but the valuation ratios panel shows net_margin=0.028 (2.8%) as of 2025-09-30. The 4.0% figure is arithmetically correct for Q2 net income/revenue, but the report also separately states 'Net margin of 2.8% is low for the trailing period' without clarifying these are different measures (quarterly vs. TTM or trailing). The TTM net income of $21.93B / TTM revenue of $693.15B = 3.16%, not 2.8%. The 2.8% figure from the valuation panel likely uses a different denominator or trailing window. The report should clarify which net margin figure corresponds to which calculation basis to avoid reader confusion.
corrections.required_action: Add a brief note distinguishing the Q2 quarterly net margin (4.0%) from the trailing/valuation-panel net margin (2.8%), and note the TTM implied margin (~3.2%) differs from both.

corrections.issue: Quant report states 'Q4 2024 (Jan 31) EPS was -$1.80' and flags this as evidence of volatility or potential one-time charges. The raw data confirms eps=-1.8 for 2024-01-31, but net_income for that quarter was $5.68B (positive). A negative EPS with positive net income is unusual and likely reflects a large share-count adjustment or dilution event, not an operating loss. The report's framing as 'earnings volatility' or 'potential one-time charges' may be misleading without noting this discrepancy.
corrections.required_action: Note that the Q4 2024 negative EPS (-$1.80) coexists with positive net income ($5.68B), suggesting a share-count or dilution-related anomaly rather than an operating loss, and flag this as a data quality issue for the PM to investigate.

corrections.issue: The qual report cites '25% e-commerce growth' from a 2025-08-22 source and '10,750+ stores, ~$681B in revenue' from the 10-K. The 10-K confirms 10,750+ stores and $681.0B in total revenues for fiscal 2025. However, the 25% e-commerce growth figure comes from a third-party news summary (indexbox.io) and is not directly verified in the 10-K text provided. This is acceptable sourcing but the PM should note it is a media-reported figure, not a direct filing disclosure in the supplied evidence.
corrections.required_action: No action required; this is a minor sourcing transparency note. The claim is consistent with the raised guidance narrative but is media-sourced, not filing-sourced.

unsupported_claims: > The quant report's TTM net margin of 2.8% does not reconcile: TTM net income $21.93B / TTM revenue $693.15B = 3.16%, not 2.8%. The 2.8% figure appears sourced from the valuation ratios panel (net_margin=0.028) but the report does not explain the discrepancy or which trailing window the valuation panel uses.

The quant report states 'Total liabilities to equity was 1.69x as of 2025-01-31 ($163.40B / $97.42B).' The raw data confirms total_liabilities=163.40B and total_equity=97.42B for that date, so the ratio is arithmetically correct. However, for the most recent quarter (2025-07-31), total_liabilities is reported as -96.55B (negative), which appears to be a data artifact (likely total_equity was stored in the total_liabilities field). The report uses the 2025-01-31 figure without noting the data quality issue in the latest balance sheet.

pm_decision_obj.executive_summary: Walmart is a dominant, operationally improving retailer with strong e-commerce momentum and a resilient balance sheet, but the stock is trading at a historically stretched valuation (P/E 42.6x at the top of its 365-day range) that largely prices in the positive narrative, leaving limited margin of safety for new positions over a one-year horizon.
pm_decision_obj.investment_thesis: Walmart is a dominant, operationally improving retailer with strong e-commerce momentum and a resilient balance sheet, but the stock is trading at a historically stretched valuation (P/E 42.6x at the top of its 365-day range) that largely prices in the positive narrative, leaving limited margin of safety for new positions over a one-year horizon.
key_factors: Valuation is stretched at the top of its historical range: P/E of 42.58 (vs. 365-day range 28.8–43.1) and P/B of 9.18 (vs. range 6.8–9.3) as of 2025-09-30, compressing forward return potential after a +29.0% annual price gain.

Strong operational momentum: Q2 2025 revenue of $177.4B (+7.1% QoQ), diluted EPS of $0.88 (+57.1% QoQ), and 25% e-commerce growth reported on 2025-08-22, with management raising full-year guidance on 2025-08-21.

Low net margins and earnings quality concerns: TTM net margin is approximately 3.2% (TTM net income $21.93B / TTM revenue $693.15B), and the Q4 2024 negative EPS of -$1.80 coexists with positive net income of $5.68B, suggesting a share-count anomaly rather than an operating loss but still indicating data complexity.

Material external headwinds: tariff cost absorption noted by the CFO on 2025-08-22, decelerating U.S. household spending per Numerator on 2025-09-16, and legal exposure including a $5.5M consumer protection settlement on 2025-08-13, partially offset by Walmart's scale-driven pricing power and EDLP strategy.
